item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The description loses any sense of tension with confusing sub-designations & the interview log is hammy & melodramatic

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great concept, tho there's just too many runon sentences. Neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Both valid points. I was a little worried myself that the former of those was confusing, but nobody else had pointed it out so I didn't worry that much. I like your rewording to emphasise medical history so I'll definitely include that, but I might add some dialogue to make it explicit that's why she survived.

I'm also glad you mentioned the "not a lot of words" - maybe it's been from a long history of clinical writing IRL, but even in fiction I like to convey as much as possible in as little as possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit the picture isn't anything inspired; it's meant to just be an unsettling image of 3544-1 instances to help the reader picture what they look like. It worked well for other articles that inspired me, such as SCP-3199 and SCP-2852, and there are dozens of other articles featuring images for the sake of portraying an anomaly, so I didn't really think there was anything wrong with it.

I suppose if you strip everything back the entire anomaly is "shadow people invade your house, cut out your organs and take your soul", but there are a lot of themes here I've tried to really reinforce to take it beyond that. The idea of a home, your safe space, being corrupted and preventing you from escape is one of those, which I compounded with the partner being the perpetrator of violence. The organs removed are significant too: each one corresponds to a chakra gate, so the removal of organs is a process of separating the victim's soul from their body. Finally, there's an underlying theme of domestic violence - a shadow possessing your loved one, hurting you as they claim to not want to, and preventing you leaving. The 76% of 3544-B cases being male was deliberate, too, and was supposed to reflect figures from 2014, which was the latest I could get an accurate number on. Maybe it's tacky and/or my writing skill wasn't enough to pull it off, but I did try to take this skip beyond generic gore, or "standard creepypasta" as you put it.

item.title: Re: Credits
item.content:encoded: I think in these cases it just feels like the author doesn't want to settle on a number. The number doesn't matter but why would it even be expunged except to try to creep us out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dang this packs a good hard punch in not a lot of words, nice. Just wanted to clarify a few things though:

Once the target is incapacitated, often in contrast to their vocalizations of protest

Is the "their" the target or SCP-3544-B? From the log I assume it's the latter but it wasn't too clear for me on the first read through.

Also to add on to what Modern_Erasmus said above, I too thought the SCP had moved the heart because I thought the diagnosis was a post-event Foundation one. Maybe a wording like "Further investigation into POI-3544-01's medical history revealed that she had previously been diagnosed with dextrocardia, a condition where the heart is on the right side of the chest." would be clearer?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I see. I thought it was implying that the instance had moved her heart rather than taking it, which is why I was confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you liked it!
The POI survived because she had dextrocardia; her heart was on the other side of her chest. The creatures weren't able to locate her heart, and thus weren't able to finish the ritual. I tried to imply this both in the closing statement, and in the interview where the POI notes she felt a hand scraping inside the left side of her chest. You're the second person to mention they didn't get the ending though, so I might make it a bit more explicit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good stuff. Sorry if I’m being dense and missed a clue (it’s early and I need my coffee) but why did this POI survive while all others didn’t?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neither have I.

It's just, you've got a picture of vaguely human shadows emerging from fog. How does that represent what happens in the article at all? Who took that image? Then all that happens is weird shadow people sneak into your house, tie you up, and harvest your organs. It's not bad, per se, but it's not terribly interesting either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the interview catch!
I'm sorry you thought this was too "standard creepypasta" - I never got into 4chan culture, so could you define exactly what that is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything about this is very "standard creepypasta", helped in no way by that image.

You've got an XX in the interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback!
An initial draft mentioned that the surgical removal demonstrated skill far beyond any training the possessed individual had received, but in this case I imagined the MTF squad rescued her and the foundation stitched her back together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I enjoyed it, but I do have a question:

pineal gland

You realize this is deep inside the heart of the brain, correct? I'm wondering how the woman in the interview survived that (besides the heart, everything else you can more or less survive without) or really in general how anyone is supposed to "extract" it from a conscious victim using household tools without essentially bashing the head open. At which point, even if the victim remains anomalously conscious, there wouldn't be much of a forehead left to touch for the final act.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this when it was in draft, still love it now.

"The screaming stopped, but when I opened my eyes he was still standing there, half his face gone, his left eye hanging out of his socket…" - I find the thought of opening your eyes and being faced with this absolutely horrifying. In my opinion, up until that point the interview log does a good job of leading you to believe POI-3544-1 survives due to fighting off the -B instance.

I think the domestic violence imagery - your partner being possessed by a dark shadow, being trapped in your house - is also well executed and effective.

+1

item.title: Re: Credits
item.content:encoded: I liked the idea of readers substituting redactions for what they considered the most terrifying - do you find the idea of just 5 entities scarier, because it gives it a personal touch? Or do you think a large number really reinforces a feeling of helplessness? The time was meant to be similar - how long are these creatures looking over you before you wake up? I personally found that to be more horrifying than giving distinct numbers, but you're the second person now to mention they felt the redaction unnecessary, so it seems like I missed the mark. I'll probably remove them.

item.title: Re: Credits
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed reading this tho I feel it was unnecessary to expunge the amount of entities that generally appear and how much time it takes for them to wake up

item.title: Credits
item.content:encoded:
Image credits:
Michel Moreau - Shadow https://www.flickr.com/photos/xmaes/8272321650/

Crit Credits:
amnestic_protocol - my best crit buddy, for all reviews at all stages of the skip.
Lixerf - Helping me rewrite a generic torture skip into something more meaningful.
Eskobar - Great veteran advice and helping me with redactions.
Dysadron - Detailed feedback on the final draft.
And everyone else who reviewed my draft over the last week!
